paper_title: Promoting strength-ductility synergy by heterogeneous grain structure in a zirconium-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel
citation_sentences: To further reveal the microstructure evolution of the Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels, the EBSD analysis was conducted and the results are shown in Fig. 2. For the Zr0 steel, the EBSD data including inverse pole figure (IPF), image quality (IQ) and kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps (Fig. 2a–a ) reveal that its microstructure has equiaxed FCC grains with low KAM values. Further adding micro-alloyed Zr in the Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steel, the average size of recrystallized FCC grains is

obviously reduced and their grain orientations are also random, as shown in Fig. 2b–b2. Differently, Fig. 2c–c2 reveals that the micro structure exhibits a distinct heterostructure including CG zones and FG zones. Compared to the CG zones with low KAM values, the FG zones exhibit a high KAM value, indicating a high density of dislocations are decorated in these fine FCC grains.

where $f _ { \mathrm { F G } }$ and $f _ { \mathrm { C G } }$ are fractions of FGs and $\mathbf { C G s } ,$ respectively, $f _ { \mathrm { C G , b } }$ is the fraction of those CGs adjacent to FGs, which is \~37 % based on EBSD result (Fig. 2). σFG and σCG can be estimated using Hall-Petch equation [26]:

caption: Fig. 3. (a) Area fraction of FG and CG zones in Zr0, Zr0.01 and Zr0.50 steels. (b)–(d) Grain size distribution of FCC grains in Zr0, Zr0.01 and Zr0.50 steels, respectively.
paper_abstract: Yuheng Wang , Runze Yu , Fengchao An \* , Suotao Wang \*\*, Hao Wang , Zibo Zhao , Hongpu Yue , Tianxiang Gao , Junting Luo \*\*\*, Junsong Zhang , Riping Liu
paper_title: Promoting strength-ductility synergy by heterogeneous grain structure in a zirconium-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel
citation_sentences: According to the BSE images and EBSD data, more microstructural details of CG zones and FG zones, including their average sizes and area fraction, are estimated and shown in Fig. 3. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fraction of fine grains is significantly increased (0 % → 35 %), as increasing Zr content from 0 wt% to 0.50 wt %. For the average size of FCC grains (Fig. 3b–d), the average size of FCC grains is obviously reduced (12 μm → 7 μm) when the steel is added to 0.01 wt % Zr. Further adding Zr into the steel (i.e., Zr0.50 steel), the FCC grains exhibit a bimodal distribution of 5 μm and 1 μm, resulting from the heterogeneous structure including CG zones and FG zones, which stands in contrast to the homogeneous FCC grains in Zr0 steel and Zr0.01 steel. These results suggest that increasing Zr content can lead to a heterogeneous architecture of FCC matrix.

In general, heterogeneous grain structures, e.g., bimodal recrystallized grains (CG/FG zones) [9], recrystallized and non-recrystallized zones [13], are designed through introducing partial recrystallization. The TMT, i.e., severe cold-rolling and various annealing conditions, is a significant approach for the partial recrystallization. In present work, a typical TMT treatment including 80 % cold-rolling and short-time annealing $( 9 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 min) was applied in the Fe-Mn-Al-C steels. However, the Zr0 steel exhibits a homogeneous grain structure after the TMT treatment. Interestingly, the combination of TMT treatment and Zr addition leads to a heterogeneous grain structure including CG and FG zones. As increasing Zr content, the fraction of FG zones is obviously increased (Fig. 3). Thus, it is necessary to reveal the formation mechanism of heterogeneous grain structure in Zr0.50 steel.

where $l _ { \mathrm { H b a r } }$ is the width of hetero-boundary affected region, which is calculated by $[ 1 - \left( \sigma _ { C G } - \sigma _ { F G } \right) ^ { 2 } ] d _ { C G }$ . Based on Fig. 3, the $l _ { \mathrm { H b a r } }$ is estimated to be 2.45 μm. Based on Equations (2)–(4), the strengthening contributions from grain boundary and HDI are \~300 MPa.

where M is the Taylor factor (3.06), SFE is the stacking faults energy, b is Burgers' vector (0.147 nm) and D is the grain size (Fig. 3). In present work, the SFE of FCC matrix in Zr0.50 steel is estimated to be \~55 mJ/ m2 (Supplementary text S1). Thus, the critical twinning stress of the Zr0.50 steel is \~1.14 GPa, which is obviously lower than its true ultimate tensile strength (the inset in Fig. 4a). Such high strengthening ef fect promotes the formation of deformation twinning, thereby enabling the further strain hardening capability. Thus, the synergic effects of HDI hardening and deformation twins provide a reasonable strain hardening rate, which further improves the fracture ductility.

paper_title: Promoting strength-ductility synergy by heterogeneous grain structure in a zirconium-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel
citation_sentences: To reveal the strain partitioning behavior in the hetero-structured Zr0.50 steel, the von Mises maps are obtained from quasi in-situ μ-DIC as a function of global strain in Fig. 5. The EBSD-IQ map (Fig. 5a) shows the measured area comprising the coarse FCC grains and fine FCC grains. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, significant strain partitioning is observed at a minor plastic strain of 2 %. The CG zones and hetero-interfaces between FG and CG zones experiences high strain, whereas the FG zones remain nearly undeformed. This is mainly attributed to the hardness differences between CG zones (soft regions) and FG zones (hard regions). As the global strain increased to 8 % (Fig. 5c), the local strain of the FG zones becomes more pronounced, indicating that the strain partitioning between soft and hard regions persists during plastic deformation. Such strain partitioning behavior leads to the generation of geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs), promoting a heterogeneous

Except for the enhanced yield strength, the Zr0.50 steel exhibits a remarkable strain hardening capability and fracture ductility, as shown in Fig. 4a. For the heterogeneous grain structure, a high density of GNDs is generated during the elasto-plastic co-deformation, as evidenced in Fig. 5b. As the global strain increasing, the co-deformation of soft CG zones and hard FG zones occurs, which further promotes the compatible deformation and induces additional strain hardening, which is expressed as [29]:

caption: Fig. 6. Microstructure characterization of the Zr0.50 steel during tensile deformation. (a) TEM imaging shows a planar slip in FCC grain interior after a minor deformation. (b) TEM imaging reveals the slip bands after a severe deformation. (c) TEM imaging displaying a high density of nanotwins (indicated by red arrows). (c1) HRTEM imaging showing a nanoscale nanotwin. (d) TEM imaging showing the interaction between dislocations and nanoscale ZrC particles. (d1) FFT pattern and HRTEM image confirm the ZrC carbide. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
paper_abstract: Yuheng Wang , Runze Yu , Fengchao An \* , Suotao Wang \*\*, Hao Wang , Zibo Zhao , Hongpu Yue , Tianxiang Gao , Junting Luo \*\*\*, Junsong Zhang , Riping Liu
paper_title: Promoting strength-ductility synergy by heterogeneous grain structure in a zirconium-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel
citation_sentences: To grain deeper insights into the deformation mechanisms of the Zr0.50 steel, TEM/HRTEM based techniques were carried out. Fig. 6a shows that dislocations as a vital deformation mechanism are initially activated in FCC matrix during a minor of plastic deformation. At the fracture surface, the severe deformed FCC matrix (Fig. 6b) exhibits a high density of slip bands along 111planes, which generally leads to a strengthening effect [18]. Excepted for dislocations, nanotwins with \~20 nm in size are generated during a heavy deformation, as evidenced by a TEM imaging (Fig. 6c). In the meantime, a high density of dislocation is decorated around the nanotwinned region, which contributes to the interaction between dislocations and nanotwins. Such behavior further effectively reduces the mean free paths of mobile dislocations, thereby leading to a typical twinning-induced plasticity. Fig. 6c reveals that twin boundaries are decorated by multiple stacking faults, which

indicates that the nanotwins formation originates from the transition from multiple Shockley partial dislocations to twins [19]. In addition to the deformation behaviors of FCC matrix, Fig. 6d shows that numerous particles with \~30 nm in size are distributed in FCC grain interior, which can effectively inhibit the movement of dislocations, i.e., precipitation strengthening. A detail analysis on these nanoscale particles via HRTEM and corresponding FFT pattern (Fig. 6d ) confirms that these nanosized particles are identified as ZrC carbides, due to the addition of Zr and the strong affinity between Zr and C [20].

For the annealed Zr0.50 steel, Zr elements are mainly distributed in FCC matrix, except for precipitation of ZrC. Based on Equation (1), Zr in solid solution and ZrC precipitation exhibit a retarding effect of recrystallization. Specifically, these Zr elements distributed in FCC matrix serve as solute atoms, which usually diffuses to the vicinity of defects including grain boundaries, shear bands and dislocations. During annealing process, the accumulation of these Zr atoms in turn impedes the movement of grain boundaries and recovery of shear bands and dislocations. Such drag effect from Zr atoms leads to a high energy barrier of recrystallization of cold-rolled microstructure during annealing [11,22], thereby inducing the partial recrystallization. Further, according to the thermodynamic calculation (Fig. 7), the fraction of ZrC particles is \~0.6 % after annealing at 900 ◦C. These nanoscale ZrC particles also inhibit the migration of grain boundaries during recrystallization through a typical Zener pinning effect. The Zener pinning force is usually related to the average size of precipitates and fraction of precipitates [23]. Compared to the Zr0 and Zr0.01 steels, the fraction of ZrC carbides in Zr0.50 steel is highest. Based on Fig. 6d, the size of ZrC in the Zr0.50 steel is only 30 nm. As a result, these nanoscale ZrC carbides in the Zr0.50 steel can lead to a desirable Zener pinning force, thereby playing a pivotal role of the partial recrystallization. Additionally, the micro shear bands caused by the heavy cold-rolling deformation are the preferred sites for the nucleation of recrystallization $[ 2 4 , 2 5 ]$ , which provides the enough driving force to formation of fine grains. As a result, the synergetic effects of drag effect of Zr atoms, Zener pinning of ZrC and TMT treatment promote the heterogeneous grain structure in the Zr0.50 steel.

caption: Fig. 7. Thermodynamic phase fraction calculation for the Zr0.50 steel using the Thermo-Calc software.

Based on Equation (1), Zr in solid solution and ZrC precipitation exhibit a retarding effect of recrystallization. Specifically, these Zr elements distributed in FCC matrix serve as solute atoms, which usually diffuses to the vicinity of defects including grain boundaries, shear bands and dislocations. During annealing process, the accumulation of these Zr atoms in turn impedes the movement of grain boundaries and recovery of shear bands and dislocations. These nanoscale ZrC particles also inhibit the migration of grain boundaries during recrystallization through a typical Zener pinning effect. Compared to the Zr0 and Zr0.01 steels, the fraction of ZrC carbides in Zr0.50 steel is highest. Based on Fig. 6d, the size of ZrC in the Zr0.50 steel is only 30 nm.
